context.project_description: Build token usage tracking for LLM operations across all supported providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) to enable cost monitoring and optimization in the Fitz KRAG framework.
key_requirements: Add token usage tracking that works across all provider types (chat, embedding, vision)
Aggregate token counts per-query for practical cost visibility
Integrate with existing instrumentation system without breaking current APIs
constraints: Must integrate with existing provider protocols (ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider)
No breaking changes to current APIs or functionality
Works across all supported LLM backends in the codebase
stakeholders: Developers who need accurate, non-intrusive tracking that doesn't break existing workflows
Operations team members who want clear visibility into LLM costs for budget management and optimization

in_scope: Token counting for LLM calls: chat(), embed(), describe_image() methods
Support all current LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama)
out_of_scope: Real-time streaming token counting during chat_stream operations

existing_files: fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — Core hook system for benchmarking and caching
fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py — Protocol interfaces that all LLM providers implement
fitz_ai/engines/fitz_krag/engine.py — Main query processing layer where LLM calls are made
needed_artifacts: fitz_ai/core/token_tracker.py — Centralized token tracking logic with thread-local context management, aggregation functions, and integration with instrumentation hooks for Answer metadata injection

assumptions.assumption: All LLM calls go through protocol interfaces (ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, VisionProvider)
assumptions.impact: If true, we can instrument all calls at the interface level; if false, we may miss direct HTTP/SDK calls that don't use these protocols

assumptions.assumption: Streaming support is not required for MVP and query-level totals are sufficient
assumptions.impact: If streaming becomes important later, we'll need to refactor the token counting logic to handle real-time updates during streams

assumptions.assumption: Providers return token counts from API responses (most modern LLM APIs provide usage info in response metadata)
assumptions.impact: If providers don't return exact counts, we'll need to implement estimation logic which could introduce inaccuracies and complexity

assumptions.assumption: The instrumentation system can be leveraged with InstrumentedProxy for wrapping existing providers without breaking changes or creating new provider files
assumptions.impact: If incorrect, we may end up violating the no-breaking-changes constraint or not being able to integrate cleanly with existing infrastructure

approaches.name: Enhanced Provider Wrapping with Context Tracking
approaches.description: Create a layered approach using context managers to collect token usage without breaking existing provider interfaces. This involves wrapping providers with usage-aware versions that capture SDK response data and store it in a shared context.
pros: Maintains backward compatibility with existing provider contracts
Leverages existing instrumentation system with minimal modifications
cons: Requires creating wrapper factories for each provider type
May add complexity to the provider instantiation flow
Needs careful handling of different SDK usage structures across providers
best_for: Large codebases with established provider contracts

approaches.name: Adapter Pattern for Token-Aware Providers
approaches.description: Create adapter classes that extend existing providers to collect token usage while preserving the original interface contract. Each adapter wraps a specific provider implementation and extracts usage data from SDK responses.
pros: Preserves exact same API contracts as current implementations
Clean separation of concerns between core logic and usage tracking
Easily testable with individual adapters for each provider type
cons: Requires creating multiple adapter classes (one per concrete provider)
Slightly more code duplication across different provider types
May require changes to how providers are instantiated in factory patterns
best_for: When exact API compatibility is critical
Projects with few LLM providers that can be easily adapted

architecture.recommended: Enhanced Provider Wrapping with Context Tracking
architecture.reasoning: This approach is recommended because it addresses the core constraint of maintaining backward compatibility while solving all identified challenges. The solution avoids modifying interface contracts (which would break existing code) and works within the established instrumentation framework. It specifically tackles Issue 1 by not requiring changes to ChatProvider.chat(), EmbeddingProvider.embed() etc., Issue 2 by extending the instrumentation system's capabilities, and Issue 3 by designing a clean path for accumulating usage data in the Answer model through the engine pipeline.

key_tradeoffs.Backward Compatibility vs. Interface Enh: Must preserve existing provider interfaces (no breaking changes) by using adapters/wrappers instead of modifying core contracts, which adds complexity but maintains compatibility.
key_tradeoffs.Performance Impact vs. Granularity: Minimal overhead since only recording metrics once per call, not during streaming or batch processing; acceptable latency for accurate token tracking.
key_tradeoffs.Provider Consistency vs. Abstraction Lay: Different SDKs have different usage structures (OpenAI vs Cohere), handled by abstraction layer that normalizes data formats without requiring uniform interfaces.

technology_considerations: Python context managers for managing short-lived usage contexts
Instrumentation system extension to capture and extract usage metadata from SDK responses
Dataclasses for Answer model with optional token_usage field
Decorator-based wrapping patterns for provider augmentation without interface modification
Hook-based instrumentation approach to integrate seamlessly with existing benchmarking infrastructure
architecture.scope_statement: Add token usage tracking capability that captures LLM provider consumption metrics without breaking existing APIs or requiring changes to core provider interfaces. This involves extending the instrumentation system, updating answer models with usage fields, and integrating tracking into the main engine pipeline.

adrs.title: ADR: Token Usage Tracking Architecture
adrs.context: Add token usage tracking so I can see how many LLM tokens each query costs. This requires capturing and aggregating token consumption from all LLM provider calls throughout the execution pipeline.
adrs.decision: Implement a layered approach using enhanced instrumentation with context tracking rather than modifying core provider interfaces, creating adapter patterns for token-aware providers, and extending the Answer model to store usage data.
adrs.rationale: This solution preserves backward compatibility by not breaking existing provider contracts while enabling comprehensive token tracking. It leverages existing infrastructure like InstrumentedProxy and BenchmarkHook systems but extends them to capture SDK response metadata including usage fields. The approach uses context managers for clean state management and avoids performance impact through minimal overhead during method calls.
consequences: All LLM providers continue returning their current data types (str, list[float], etc.) without breaking changes
Token usage data is captured transparently via instrumentation system extensions
Answer model now supports token_usage field for reporting
New UsageContext class manages tracking state per-query execution
Engine methods can optionally accept and use usage context to accumulate metrics
alternatives_considered: Alternative 1 - Modify provider interfaces to return tuples with tokens: rejected because it would break backward compatibility with existing code that depends on current method signatures like ChatProvider.chat() returning str

Alternative 2 - Direct modification of all provider implementations to capture and store usage data locally: rejected because it creates duplication across multiple providers, is hard to maintain, and doesn't leverage existing instrumentation framework properly

adrs.title: ADR: Centralized UsageContext Storage vs Distributed Context Propagation
adrs.context: The architecture uses a centralized UsageContext that gets passed through the instrumentation system, which means all context tracking relies on this single point of truth rather than distributed context propagation. This creates tight coupling between wrapper components and the context storage mechanism.
adrs.decision: Use a global/centralized UsageContext with thread-local or request-scoped storage to maintain state across wrapped provider calls
adrs.rationale: This approach provides clean separation of concerns while maintaining backward compatibility with existing interfaces. It allows for centralized monitoring without requiring interface modifications and works within the established instrumentation framework.
consequences: Tight coupling between wrapper components and context management system creates a single point of failure if the global context storage becomes corrupted or unavailable
Thread-local storage may not work correctly in async/await scenarios or when using thread pools, potentially leading to context leakage or incorrect usage tracking
Difficult to debug and trace across distributed systems where context needs to be propagated via HTTP headers or other mechanisms
alternatives_considered: Distributed context propagation with explicit context passing through method parameters - would break backward compatibility but provide better distributed tracing support
Context injection via decorator pattern at call site - would require code changes and could be error-prone during adoption
Event-based context tracking using message queues or pub/sub systems - provides maximum decoupling but adds significant complexity and latency

components.name: UsageContext
components.purpose: Manages and aggregates token usage data from LLM provider calls
responsibilities: Track prompt and completion tokens by provider and method
Store usage data in a structured format for reporting
interfaces: record_usage(provider_name: str, method_name: str, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int)

components.name: TokenUsageHook
components.purpose: Instrumentation hook that extracts token usage from LLM SDK responses
responsibilities: Capture usage data during provider method calls
Store extracted usage in associated UsageContext
interfaces: on_call_start(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> Any
on_call_end(context: Any, result: Any, error: Exception | None) -> None

components.name: TokenAwareOpenAIChat
components.purpose: Adapter that wraps OpenAIChat to track token usage without breaking interface contract
responsibilities: Call original OpenAIChat methods while capturing usage data
Store usage in associated UsageContext

components.name: TokenAwareOpenAIEmbedding
components.purpose: Adapter that wraps OpenAIEmbedding to track token usage without breaking interface contract
responsibilities: Call original OpenAIEmbedding methods while capturing usage data
Store usage in associated UsageContext

components.name: TokenAwareCohereChat
components.purpose: Adapter that wraps CohereChat to track token usage without breaking interface contract
responsibilities: Call original CohereChat methods while capturing usage data
Store usage in associated UsageContext

components.name: TokenAwareAnthropicChat
components.purpose: Adapter that wraps AnthropicChat to track token usage without breaking interface contract
responsibilities: Call original AnthropicChat methods while capturing usage data
Store usage in associated UsageContext

components.name: FitzKragEngine
components.purpose: Main engine for knowledge routing augmented generation with token usage tracking capability
responsibilities: Execute query processing pipeline including LLM calls
Optionally collect and aggregate token usage data during execution
Return answers enriched with token usage information when requested
interfaces: answer(query: Query, usage_context: Optional[UsageContext] = None) -> Answer

artifacts.purpose: Provides usage tracking context and hook system for collecting LLM token consumption data
Extends the Answer model to include token usage information for tracking LLM consumption
Modifies the engine's answer method to accept and utilize usage context for collecting token metrics

artifacts.content:
# ... existing imports ...
from fitz_ai.core.usage_tracker import UsageContext
# ... rest of file remains unchanged except for modifications below ...

class fitz:
    # ... existing methods ...
    
    def ask(self, question: str, top_k: Optional[int], conversation_context: Optional['ConversationContext'], track_usage: bool = False) -> Answer:
        usage_context = UsageContext() if track_usage else None
        answer = self._ask(question, top_k, conversation_context)
        if track_usage and usage_context:
            answer.token_usage = dict(usage_context.usage_by_provider)
        return answer
    
    def query(self, question: str, conversation_context: Optional['ConversationContext'], track_usage: bool = False) -> Answer:
        usage_context = UsageContext() if track_usage else None
        answer = self._query(question, conversation_context)
        if track_usage and usage_context:
            answer.token_usage = dict(usage_context.usage_by_provider)
        return answer
artifacts.purpose: Updates SDK methods to optionally collect and return token usage data from queries

adrs.context: Add token usage tracking so I can see how many LLM tokens each query costs. This requires capturing and aggregating token consumption from all LLM provider calls throughout the execution pipeline.
adrs.decision: Implement a layered approach using enhanced instrumentation with context tracking rather than modifying core provider interfaces, creating adapter patterns for token-aware providers, and extending the Answer model to store usage data.
adrs.rationale: This solution preserves backward compatibility by not breaking existing provider contracts while enabling comprehensive token tracking. This creates tight coupling between wrapper components and the context storage mechanism.
adrs.decision: Use a global/centralized UsageContext with thread-local or request-scoped storage to maintain state across wrapped provider calls

phases.name: Read Before Writing
phases.objective: Verify integration points by reading source
phases.verification_command: pytest fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_base.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_openai.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_anthropic.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_cohere.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_ollama.py

phases.name: Core Token Tracker Implementation
phases.objective: Implement a thread-local usage aggregator for tracking LLM tokens consumed during a single request/query execution
key_risks: Thread-local storage may not work correctly in async contexts
Token extraction patterns vary across different LLM providers
phases.verification_command: python -c "from fitz_ai.core.token_tracker import UsageContext, get_usage_context; ctx = get_usage_context(); print('Token tracking initialized successfully'); ctx.record_usage('openai', 'chat', 100, 200); assert ctx.total_tokens == 300; print('Usage recording works correctly')"

phases.name: Instrumentation Integration
phases.objective: Integrate token tracking with existing LLM provider infrastructure using InstrumentedProxy to intercept real API responses and extract actual usage data
deliverables: Updated provider factory logic to wrap providers
key_risks: Different SDKs have varying response structures for token usage data
Performance overhead from wrapping all LLM calls with instrumentation
Need to handle different method signatures across providers

risks.description: Token usage extraction logic may fail to parse usage data from different LLM provider SDK responses due to inconsistent response formats across providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, etc.)
risks.mitigation: Implement robust error handling with fallbacks and comprehensive unit tests for each supported provider's usage format parsing
risks.contingency: Fall back to zero token counts when extraction fails, log warning messages to alert operators of potential tracking issues
risks.verification: assert that all providers return valid token data or gracefully handle missing/invalid usage fields

risks.description: Thread-local storage used for context isolation may cause incorrect aggregation when concurrent queries are processed in the same thread, leading to inaccurate cost attribution per query
risks.mitigation: Implement rigorous testing with multi-threaded scenarios and ensure proper reset of usage contexts after each request
risks.contingency: Add explicit context cleanup on request completion and implement thread-safe access checks for token data during concurrent execution
risks.verification: assert that each query maintains isolated token counts in multi-threaded environments

risks.description: Instrumentation overhead from wrapping all LLM provider calls could impact performance, especially under high concurrency or with large numbers of LLM operations per request
risks.mitigation: Profile token tracking implementation and use conditional instrumentation only when enabled via configuration flag
risks.contingency: Provide toggleable feature flag to disable token tracking in production environments where performance is critical, defaulting to disabled state
risks.verification: assert that latency increases less than 5% under normal load conditions

risks.description: Token counts may be inaccurate or missing if the wrapping logic fails to intercept certain provider methods or response types, particularly in streaming operations or custom integrations
risks.mitigation: Maintain comprehensive method tracking list and validate against actual SDK usage patterns for all supported LLM providers
risks.contingency: Log discrepancy warnings when token data is not captured from expected methods, implement fallback mechanisms to track basic operations even if detailed usage isn't available
risks.verification: assert that all documented provider methods are properly wrapped and tracked

risks.description: Instrumentation logic might inadvertently expose sensitive data (like tokens or API keys) in logs if not carefully implemented, particularly when tracing internal method calls
risks.mitigation: Apply strict logging filters to prevent inclusion of PII in traces and sanitize all instrumentation output
risks.contingency: Implement comprehensive log sanitization rules for instrumentation layer and conduct regular security reviews of traced data flows
risks.verification: assert that logs do not contain sensitive information from LLM requests or responses

recommended_contingencies: Implement conditional instrumentation with feature flag to disable tracking in performance-sensitive environments
Add comprehensive error logging and monitoring for failed token extraction scenarios with alerting thresholds
Create dedicated test suite specifically for multi-threaded usage context isolation under concurrent load
